question: 7. Given a sequence \( \left\{a_{n}\right\} \) of non-negative real numbers such that \( a_{n+m} \leq \) \( a_{n} a_{m} \) for all pairs of positive integers, \( m \) and \( n \), prove that the sequence \( \left\{\sqrt[n]{a_{n}}\right\} \) has a limit as \( n \rightarrow \infty \).
solution:
Solution. If \( a_{p}=0 \) for some \( p \), then from \( a_{p+m} \leq 0 \cdot a_{m} \) we have \( a_{p+1} \) \( =0, a_{p+2}=0, \ldots \), and indeed \( a_{n}=0 \) for all \( n \geq p \). In this event \( \lim a_{n}{ }^{1 / n}=0 \).

If no \( a_{p}=0 \), then \( a_{n}>0 \) for all \( n \), and we can put
\[
b_{n}=\log a_{n} .
\]

Then the given inequality becomes
\[
b_{m+n} \leq b_{m}+b_{n}
\]
and it follows that
\[
b_{k m+t} \leq k b_{m}+b_{t}
\]
for any non-negative integers \( k, m, t \).
We fix the positive integer \( m \), and for any \( n \), let \( n=k(n) m+t(n) \) where \( 0 \leq t(n)<m \). Let \( c=\max \left(b_{0}, b_{1}, \ldots, b_{m-1}\right) \)
\[
\begin{aligned}
b_{n}=b_{k(n) m+t(n)} & \leq k(n) b_{m}+b_{t(n)} \\
& \leq k(n) b_{m}+c
\end{aligned}
\]
and hence
\[
\frac{b_{n}}{n}<\frac{k(n)}{n} b_{m}+\frac{c}{n}
\]

As \( n \rightarrow \infty, \frac{k(n)}{n} \rightarrow \frac{1}{m} \), hence
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{n} b_{n} \leq \frac{1}{m} b_{m}
\]

If \( \alpha \) is a number exceeding \( \lim \inf b_{n} / n \), we can choose \( m \) so that \( b_{m} / m \) \( <\alpha \). Then (1) shows that
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{n} b_{n} \leq \alpha
\]

Hence we conclude
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{n} b_{n} \leq \lim \inf \frac{1}{n} b_{n} .
\]

Thus, \( \lim b_{n} / n \) exists, but possibly improperly. It is less than \( b_{1} \), for we may take \( m=1 \) in (1), but it may be \( -\infty \). In any case
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} a_{n}^{1 / n}=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \exp \left(\frac{1}{n} b_{n}\right)
\]
exists.

descriptive_long.question: 7. Given a sequence \( \left\{termgeneral\right\} \) of non-negative real numbers such that \( a_{countern+counterm} \leq termgeneral\,termindexm \) for all pairs of positive integers, \( counterm \) and \( countern \), prove that the sequence \( \left\{\sqrt[countern]{termgeneral}\right\} \) has a limit as \( countern \rightarrow \infty \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. If \( termindexp =0 \) for some \( counterp \), then from \( a_{counterp+counterm} \leq 0 \cdot termindexm \) we have \( a_{counterp+1}=0, a_{counterp+2}=0, \ldots \), and indeed \( termgeneral =0 \) for all \( countern \geq counterp \). In this event \( \lim termgeneral^{1 / countern}=0 \).

If no \( termindexp =0 \), then \( termgeneral >0 \) for all \( countern \), and we can put
\[
loggeneral = \log termgeneral .
\]
Then the given inequality becomes
\[
b_{counterm+countern} \leq logindexm + loggeneral
\]
and it follows that
\[
b_{counterk\,counterm+countert} \leq counterk\,logindexm + logindext
\]
for any non-negative integers \( counterk, counterm, countert \).

We fix the positive integer \( counterm \), and for any \( countern \), let \( countern = counterk(countern)\,counterm + countert(countern) \) where \( 0 \leq countert(countern) < counterm \). Let
\[
maxbound = \max \left(logzero,\,logone,\,\ldots,\,b_{counterm-1}\right).
\]
Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
loggeneral = b_{counterk(countern)\,counterm + countert(countern)} &\leq counterk(countern)\,logindexm + b_{countert(countern)}\\[2pt]
&\leq counterk(countern)\,logindexm + maxbound,
\end{aligned}
\]
and hence
\[
\frac{loggeneral}{countern} < \frac{counterk(countern)}{countern}\,logindexm + \frac{maxbound}{countern}.
\]
As \( countern \rightarrow \infty, \; \dfrac{counterk(countern)}{countern} \rightarrow \dfrac{1}{counterm} \); hence
\[
\limsup \frac{1}{countern} \, loggeneral \leq \frac{1}{counterm}\,logindexm.
\]
If \( alphaval \) is a number exceeding \( \liminf \, loggeneral / countern \), we can choose \( counterm \) so that \( logindexm / counterm < alphaval \). Then the previous inequality shows that
\[
\limsup \frac{1}{countern} \, loggeneral \leq alphaval.
\]
Hence we conclude
\[
\limsup \frac{1}{countern} \, loggeneral \leq \liminf \frac{1}{countern} \, loggeneral.
\]
Thus, \( \lim loggeneral / countern \) exists, though possibly improperly. It is less than \( logone \) (take \( counterm = 1 \) in the earlier bound), but it may be \( -\infty \). In any case
\[
\lim_{countern \rightarrow \infty} termgeneral^{1 / countern} = \lim_{countern \rightarrow \infty} \exp\! igl(\tfrac{1}{countern}\,loggeneral\bigr)
\]
exists.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 7. Given a sequence \( \left\{cantaloupe_{pineapple}\right\} \) of non-negative real numbers such that \( a_{n+m} \leq \) \( cantaloupe_{pineapple} saxophone_{strawberry} \) for all pairs of positive integers, \( strawberry \) and \( pineapple \), prove that the sequence \( \left\{\sqrt[pineapple]{cantaloupe_{pineapple}}\right\} \) has a limit as \( pineapple \rightarrow \infty \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. If \( butterfly_{kangaroo}=0 \) for some \( kangaroo \), then from \( a_{p+m} \leq 0 \cdot saxophone_{strawberry} \) we have \( a_{p+1}=0, a_{p+2}=0, \ldots \), and indeed \( cantaloupe_{pineapple}=0 \) for all \( pineapple \geq kangaroo \). In this event \( \lim cantaloupe_{pineapple}^{1 / pineapple}=0 \).

If no \( butterfly_{kangaroo}=0 \), then \( cantaloupe_{pineapple}>0 \) for all \( pineapple \), and we can put
\[
watermelon_{pineapple}=\log cantaloupe_{pineapple} .
\]

Then the given inequality becomes
\[
b_{m+n} \leq telescope_{strawberry}+watermelon_{pineapple}
\]
and it follows that
\[
b_{k m+t} \leq porcupine telescope_{strawberry}+paintbrush_{alligator}
\]
for any non-negative integers \( porcupine, strawberry, alligator \).
We fix the positive integer \( strawberry \), and for any \( pineapple \), let \( pineapple=porcupine(pineapple) strawberry+alligator(pineapple) \) where \( 0 \leq alligator(pineapple)<strawberry \). Let \( zeppelin=\max \left(spaceship, chocolate, \ldots, b_{m-1}\right) \)
\[
\begin{aligned}
watermelon_{pineapple}=b_{k(n) m+t(n)} & \leq k(n) telescope_{strawberry}+paintbrush_{alligator(pineapple)} \\
& \leq k(n) telescope_{strawberry}+zeppelin
\end{aligned}
\]
and hence
\[
\frac{watermelon_{pineapple}}{pineapple}<\frac{k(n)}{n} telescope_{strawberry}+\frac{zeppelin}{pineapple}
\]

As \( pineapple \rightarrow \infty, \frac{k(n)}{n} \rightarrow \frac{1}{strawberry} \), hence
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{pineapple} watermelon_{pineapple} \leq \frac{1}{strawberry} telescope_{strawberry}
\]

If \( quartzite \) is a number exceeding \( \lim \inf watermelon_{pineapple} / pineapple \), we can choose \( strawberry \) so that \( telescope_{strawberry} / strawberry <quartzite \). Then (1) shows that
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{pineapple} watermelon_{pineapple} \leq quartzite
\]

Hence we conclude
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{pineapple} watermelon_{pineapple} \leq \lim \inf \frac{1}{pineapple} watermelon_{pineapple} .
\]

Thus, \( \lim watermelon_{pineapple} / pineapple \) exists, but possibly improperly. It is less than \( chocolate \), for we may take \( strawberry=1 \) in (1), but it may be \( -\infty \). In any case
\[
\lim _{pineapple \rightarrow \infty} cantaloupe_{pineapple}^{1 / pineapple}=\lim _{pineapple \rightarrow \infty} \exp \left(\frac{1}{pineapple} watermelon_{pineapple}\right)
\]
exists.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: Given a sequence \( \left\{barrenvalue\right\} \) of non-negative real numbers such that \( a_{outcomevalue+startvalue} \leq \) \( barrenvalue infertilevalue \) for all pairs of positive integers, \( startvalue \) and \( outcomevalue \), prove that the sequence \( \left\{\sqrt[outcomevalue]{barrenvalue}\right\} \) has a limit as \( outcomevalue \rightarrow \infty \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. If \( voidmetric =0 \) for some \( continuation \), then from \( a_{continuation+startvalue} \leq 0 \cdot infertilevalue \) we have \( a_{continuation+1}=0, a_{continuation+2}=0, \ldots \), and indeed \( a_{outcomevalue}=0 \) for all \( outcomevalue \geq continuation \). In this event \( \lim barrenvalue^{1 / outcomevalue}=0 \).

If no \( voidmetric =0 \), then \( a_{outcomevalue}>0 \) for all \( outcomevalue \), and we can put
\[
exponentialdatum =\log barrenvalue .
\]

Then the given inequality becomes
\[
b_{startvalue+outcomevalue} \leq exponentialitem + exponentialdatum
\]
and it follows that
\[
b_{zerocount\, startvalue+eternity} \leq zerocount\,exponentialitem + exponentialindex
\]
for any non-negative integers \( zerocount, startvalue, eternity \).
We fix the positive integer \( startvalue \), and for any \( outcomevalue \), let \( outcomevalue=zerocount(outcomevalue)\,startvalue+eternity(outcomevalue) \) where \( 0 \leq eternity(outcomevalue)<startvalue \). Let \( negativity=\max \left(exponentialzero , exponentialone , \ldots , b_{startvalue-1}\right) \)
\[
\begin{aligned}
 b_{outcomevalue}&=b_{zerocount(outcomevalue)\,startvalue+eternity(outcomevalue)} \\
 & \leq zerocount(outcomevalue)\,exponentialitem + b_{eternity(outcomevalue)} \\
 & \leq zerocount(outcomevalue)\,exponentialitem + negativity
\end{aligned}
\]
and hence
\[
\frac{b_{outcomevalue}}{outcomevalue}<\frac{zerocount(outcomevalue)}{outcomevalue}\,exponentialitem+\frac{negativity}{outcomevalue}
\]

As \( outcomevalue \rightarrow \infty, \frac{zerocount(outcomevalue)}{outcomevalue} \rightarrow \frac{1}{startvalue} \), hence
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{outcomevalue}\,b_{outcomevalue} \leq \frac{1}{startvalue}\,exponentialitem
\]

If \( endingpoint \) is a number exceeding \( \lim \inf b_{outcomevalue}/outcomevalue \), we can choose \( startvalue \) so that \( exponentialitem/startvalue < endingpoint \). Then the preceding inequality shows that
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{outcomevalue}\,b_{outcomevalue} \leq endingpoint
\]

Hence we conclude
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{outcomevalue}\,b_{outcomevalue} \leq \lim \inf \frac{1}{outcomevalue}\,b_{outcomevalue} .
\]

Thus, \( \lim b_{outcomevalue}/outcomevalue \) exists, but possibly improperly. It is less than \( exponentialitem \), for we may take \( startvalue=1 \) in the above estimate, but it may be \( -\infty \). In any case
\[
\lim _{outcomevalue \rightarrow \infty} barrenvalue^{1 / outcomevalue}=\lim _{outcomevalue \rightarrow \infty} \exp \left(\frac{1}{outcomevalue}\,exponentialdatum\right)
\]
exists.

garbled_string.question: 7. Given a sequence \( \left\{qzxwvtnp\right\} \) of non-negative real numbers such that \( a_{rmucodve+kpjazlwh} \leq qzxwvtnp hjgrksla \) for all pairs of positive integers, \( kpjazlwh \) and \( rmucodve \), prove that the sequence \( \left\{\sqrt[rmucodve]{qzxwvtnp}\right\} \) has a limit as \( rmucodve \rightarrow \infty \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. If \( xbejqotn=0 \) for some \( tsbneqor \), then from \( a_{tsbneqor+kpjazlwh} \leq 0 \cdot hjgrksla \) we have \( a_{tsbneqor+1}=0, a_{tsbneqor+2}=0, \ldots \), and indeed \( qzxwvtnp=0 \) for all \( rmucodve \geq tsbneqor \). In this event \( \lim qzxwvtnp^{1 / rmucodve}=0 \).

If no \( xbejqotn=0 \), then \( qzxwvtnp>0 \) for all \( rmucodve \), and we can put
\[
farksqud=\log qzxwvtnp .
\]

Then the given inequality becomes
\[
b_{kpjazlwh+rmucodve} \leq glimvzhy+farksqud
\]
and it follows that
\[
b_{lzjskrwp kpjazlwh+odvhwaer} \leq lzjskrwp \, glimvzhy+wcnporad
\]
for any non-negative integers \( lzjskrwp, kpjazlwh, odvhwaer \).
We fix the positive integer \( kpjazlwh \), and for any \( rmucodve \), let \( rmucodve=lzjskrwp(rmucodve) kpjazlwh+odvhwaer(rmucodve) \) where \( 0 \leq odvhwaer(rmucodve)<kpjazlwh \). Let \( vdmraqhe=\max \left(yuqslfok, inpxghat, \ldots, b_{kpjazlwh-1}\right) \)
\[
\begin{aligned}
farksqud=b_{lzjskrwp(rmucodve) kpjazlwh+odvhwaer(rmucodve)} & \leq lzjskrwp(rmucodve) \, glimvzhy+wcnporad(rmucodve) \\& \leq lzjskrwp(rmucodve) \, glimvzhy+vdmraqhe
\end{aligned}
\]
and hence
\[
\frac{farksqud}{rmucodve}<\frac{lzjskrwp(rmucodve)}{rmucodve} \, glimvzhy+\frac{vdmraqhe}{rmucodve}
\]

As \( rmucodve \rightarrow \infty, \frac{lzjskrwp(rmucodve)}{rmucodve} \rightarrow \frac{1}{kpjazlwh} \), hence
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{rmucodve} farksqud \leq \frac{1}{kpjazlwh} \, glimvzhy
\]

If \( qvnbjser \) is a number exceeding \( \lim \inf farksqud / rmucodve \), we can choose \( kpjazlwh \) so that \( glimvzhy / kpjazlwh <qvnbjser \). Then (1) shows that
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{rmucodve} farksqud \leq qvnbjser
\]

Hence we conclude
\[
\lim \sup \frac{1}{rmucodve} farksqud \leq \lim \inf \frac{1}{rmucodve} farksqud .
\]

Thus, \( \lim farksqud / rmucodve \) exists, but possibly improperly. It is less than \( inpxghat \), for we may take \( kpjazlwh=1 \) in (1), but it may be \( -\infty \). In any case
\[
\lim _{rmucodve \rightarrow \infty} qzxwvtnp^{1 / rmucodve}=\lim _{rmucodve \rightarrow \infty} \exp \left(\frac{1}{rmucodve} farksqud\right)
\]
exists.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $d\ge 2$ and denote by  
\[
\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}:=\bigl\{(n_{1},\dots ,n_{d})\; ;\; n_{i}\in\mathbb{N}_{0}\bigr\}.
\]  
Fix a family of non-negative real numbers  
\[
\bigl(x_{n}\bigr)_{\,n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}}
\qquad\bigl(\text{with the convention }x_{\mathbf{0}}:=1\bigr)
\]  
satisfying

(1)  (sub-multiplicativity)  
\[
x_{m+n}\le x_{m}\,x_{n}\qquad\forall\,m,n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d};
\]

(2)  (non-degeneracy on the coordinate axes)  
for every unit vector $e_{i}$, $1\le i\le d$, the sequence $\bigl(x_{k e_{i}}\bigr)_{k\ge 1}$ is **not** eventually $0$;

(3)  (mild exponential upper bound)  
there exists a constant $C>0$ such that  
\[
x_{n}\le C^{\,\lVert n\rVert_{1}}\qquad\forall\,n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d},
\qquad\hbox{where }\lVert n\rVert_{1}:=n_{1}+\dots +n_{d}.
\]

For a rational direction $\theta=(\theta_{1},\dots ,\theta_{d})$ with $\theta_{i}\ge 0$ and
$\theta_{1}+\dots +\theta_{d}=1$, let $s$ be any common denominator of the $\theta_{i}$.  
Whenever $k\in s\mathbb{N}$ we put $k\theta:=(k\theta_{1},\dots ,k\theta_{d})\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$.

(a)  (directional limits)  
Show that the limit  
\[
L(\theta)\ :=\ \lim_{k\to\infty}x_{k\theta}^{\,1/k}
\]
exists (it may be $0$) for every rational direction $\theta$.  
Define  
\[
L_{i}\ :=\ \lim_{k\to\infty}x_{k e_{i}}^{\,1/k}\qquad(1\le i\le d).
\]

(b)  (asymptotic upper envelope)  
Prove that  
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\;=\;
\max_{1\le i\le d}L_{i}.
\tag{$\star$}
\]

(c)  (a necessary condition for a global limit)  
Show that if the global limit  
\[
\lim_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\]
exists, then necessarily $L_{1}=L_{2}=\dots =L_{d}$.

(d)  (failure of sufficiency)  
For $d=2$ construct an explicit family $\bigl(x_{n}\bigr)$ satisfying (1)-(3) with
$L_{1}=L_{2}$ but for which the global limit in (c) does **not** exist.
Explain why this shows that the condition found in (c) is not sufficient.

Throughout you may set $\log 0:=-\infty$ and work in the extended real line
$\mathbb{R}\cup\{-\infty\}$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  
\[
b_{n}:=\log x_{n}\in\mathbb{R}\cup\{-\infty\},
\qquad\bigl(\log 0:=-\infty,\; \log 1 =0\bigr).
\]  
With the normalisation $x_{\mathbf{0}}=1$ we have $b_{\mathbf{0}}=0$.  
Assumptions (1) and (3) become

(S)\; $b_{m+n}\le b_{m}+b_{n}$  for all $m,n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$ (sub-additivity),  

(B)\; $b_{n}\le (\log C)\lVert n\rVert_{1}$ for all $n$ (linear upper bound).

Step 1.  Directional limits - part (a).  

Fix any non-zero $v\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$.  
The sequence $k\longmapsto b_{k v}$ is sub-additive, so by Fekete's lemma the limit  
\[
\beta(v):=\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{b_{k v}}{k}\in[-\infty,\infty)
\]  
exists.  
Given a rational $\theta$ choose the **primitive** integer vector $v$ with
$\theta=v/\lVert v\rVert_{1}$.  
If $k\in s\mathbb{N}$ then $k\theta=(k/s)v$, whence
\[
\frac{b_{k\theta}}{k}\;=\;\frac{b_{(k/s)v}}{(k/s)}\cdot\frac{1}{\lVert v\rVert_{1}}
\;\xrightarrow{k\to\infty}\;
\frac{\beta(v)}{\lVert v\rVert_{1}}.
\]
Exponentiating yields
\[
L(\theta)=\exp\!\Bigl(\beta(v)/\lVert v\rVert_{1}\Bigr).
\tag{1}
\]
In particular, for $v=e_{i}$ we get $L_{i}=e^{\,\beta(e_{i})}$.  
Thus the limits in (a) exist.  \hfill$\square$ (a)

Step 2.  Coordinate-wise decomposition.  

Claim.  For every $n=(n_{1},\dots ,n_{d})\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$ one has  
\[
b_{n}\le b_{n_{1}e_{1}}+\dots +b_{n_{d}e_{d}}.
\tag{2}
\]

Proof by induction on $m:=\lVert n\rVert_{1}$.  
For $m=0$ both sides equal $0$.  
Assume (2) is known for all vectors of 1-norm $<m$ and take
$n$ with $\lVert n\rVert_{1}=m\ge 1$.  
Choose an index $j$ with $n_{j}\ge 1$ and write
\[
n=(n_{1},\dots ,n_{j}-1,\dots ,n_{d})+e_{j}.
\]
Both addends are in $\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$, the first one having strictly smaller
1-norm.  Sub-additivity (S) gives
\[
b_{n}\le b_{n-e_{j}}+b_{e_{j}}
        \le\bigl[b_{(n_{1}e_{1})}+\dots +b_{(n_{j}-1)e_{j}}+\dots +b_{n_{d}e_{d}}\bigr]+b_{e_{j}}
        = b_{n_{1}e_{1}}+\dots +b_{n_{d}e_{d}},
\]
where the middle inequality uses the inductive hypothesis for $n-e_{j}$.  
Thus (2) holds for all $n$. \hfill$\square$

Step 3.  Proof of the asymptotic upper envelope - part (b).  

Let
\[
M_{\beta}:=\max_{1\le i\le d}\beta(e_{i})\quad\Bigl(=\log\!\bigl[\max_{i}L_{i}\bigr]\Bigr).
\]

(A)  The **finite** case $\;M_{\beta}>-\infty$.  

Fix $\varepsilon>0$.  
For each $i$ the limit $\beta(e_{i})$ exists, hence there are infinitely many
$k$ with $x_{k e_{i}}>0$ and
\[
b_{k e_{i}}\le k\bigl(M_{\beta}+\varepsilon/2\bigr).
\tag{3}
\]
Pick one such integer and denote it by $K_{i}\ge 1$.  
Define
\[
K_{\max}:=\max_{i}K_{i},
\qquad
M:=\max_{1\le i\le d}\;\max_{0\le r<K_{i},\;x_{r e_{i}}>0} b_{r e_{i}},
\]
which is finite by (B).  

Now let $n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$ with $\lVert n\rVert_{1}\gg 1$.
Write $n_{i}=q_{i}K_{i}+r_{i}$ with $0\le r_{i}<K_{i}$.  
Using (2) and (3),
\[
\begin{aligned}
b_{n}\ &\le \sum_{i=1}^{d} b_{n_{i}e_{i}}
      \le \sum_{i=1}^{d}\bigl[q_{i}\,b_{K_{i}e_{i}}+b_{r_{i}e_{i}}\bigr]  \\
      &\le \sum_{i=1}^{d}q_{i}K_{i}\bigl(M_{\beta}+\varepsilon/2\bigr)+dM
      \le \lVert n\rVert_{1}\bigl(M_{\beta}+\varepsilon/2\bigr)+dM.
\end{aligned}
\]
Dividing by $\lVert n\rVert_{1}$ and letting $\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty$ gives
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}\frac{b_{n}}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\le M_{\beta}+\varepsilon.
\]
Because $\varepsilon>0$ is arbitrary,
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}\frac{b_{n}}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\le M_{\beta}.
\tag{4}
\]

(B)  The **degenerate** case $\;M_{\beta}=-\infty$ (equivalently $L_{i}=0$ for all $i$).  

Choose any positive number $M_{0}>0$.  
For every $i$ there exist infinitely many $k$ with $x_{k e_{i}}>0$ and
$b_{k e_{i}}\le -kM_{0}$.  Fix one such $k$ and call it $K_{i}$.  
Repeating the preceding argument with these $K_{i}$'s yields, for all large $n$,
\[
\frac{b_{n}}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}\le -M_{0}+\frac{dM}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\;<\;-M_{0}/2.
\]
Since $M_{0}>0$ was arbitrary we deduce
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}\frac{b_{n}}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
=-\infty.
\tag{5}
\]

(C)  Lower bound in the finite case.  
Take $n=k e_{j}$ for any index $j$ with $\beta(e_{j})=M_{\beta}$.  
Then $\displaystyle \lim_{k\to\infty}\tfrac{b_{k e_{j}}}{k}=M_{\beta}$, hence
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}\frac{b_{n}}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\ge M_{\beta}.
\tag{6}
\]

Combining (4)-(6) and exponentiating we obtain in **all** cases  
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
=\max_{1\le i\le d}L_{i},
\]
which is exactly $(\star)$. \hfill$\square$ (b)

Step 4.  Necessity of equal one-dimensional limits - part (c).  

Assume the global limit 
$L:=\displaystyle\lim_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}$
exists.  
Evaluating it along the $i$-th coordinate axis ($n=k e_{i}$) gives
\[
\lim_{k\to\infty}x_{k e_{i}}^{\,1/k}=L,
\quad\text{i.e. }L_{i}=L\qquad(1\le i\le d).
\]
Thus $L_{1}=L_{2}=\dots =L_{d}$. \hfill$\square$ (c)

Step 5.  A counter-example in dimension $2$ - part (d).  

For $n=(n_{1},n_{2})\ne\mathbf{0}$ set  
\[
x_{n}:=\begin{cases}
\mathrm{e}^{\,n_{1}+n_{2}} & \text{if }n_{1}=0\ \text{or}\ n_{2}=0,\\[4pt]
1                          & \text{if }n_{1},n_{2}>0,
\end{cases}
\qquad\text{and }x_{\mathbf{0}}:=1.
\tag{7}
\]

Verification of the assumptions.  

*  (1)  A direct case distinction using (7) shows  
$x_{m+n}\le x_{m}\,x_{n}$ for all $m,n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{2}$.  

*  (2)  Along either axis $x_{(k,0)}=x_{(0,k)}=\mathrm{e}^{\,k}>0$, hence the axis
sequences are not eventually $0$.  

*  (3)  For every $n$ one has $x_{n}\le\mathrm{e}^{\,\lVert n\rVert_{1}}$.  

Directional limits.  On the axes $x_{k e_{i}}^{\,1/k}=\mathrm{e}$, so
$L_{1}=L_{2}=\mathrm{e}$.  

Global behaviour.  Along the diagonal $n=(k,k)$ we have $x_{n}=1$, hence
$x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}=1$, while along the axis $n=(k,0)$ the same
quantity equals $\mathrm{e}$.  Therefore
\[
\liminf_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
=1
\;<\;
\mathrm{e}
=
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}.
\]
The global limit fails to exist although $L_{1}=L_{2}$, so the condition found
in (c) is **not** sufficient.  \hfill$\square$ (d)

All four parts (a)-(d) are now complete. \qed

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension: the unknowns now form a d-parameter array on ℕ⁽ᵈ⁾ rather than a single sequence, forcing a multi-variable treatment.  
• Additional constraints: we must control behaviour in every lattice direction simultaneously and prove independence from the chosen path to infinity.  
• More sophisticated structures: the proof uses the semigroup ℕ⁽ᵈ⁾, multi-variable subadditivity, primitive lattice directions, and norms on ℝᵈ.  
• Deeper theory: two separate invocations of Fekete’s lemma (one along rays, one on the minima m_k) are coupled with a delicate sandwich argument to match upper and lower asymptotic slopes.  Handling the quotient b_n/‖n‖₁ demands an “ε–approximation” by thick slabs, borrowing ideas from the multidimensional version of the Subadditive Ergodic Theorem (but staying purely deterministic).  
• Multiple interacting concepts: lattice geometry, norms, directional limits, homogenisation and subadditivity all interplay.  None of these appear in the original one-dimensional setting, and simple pattern matching is hopeless—one must coordinate several advanced tools to finish the proof.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $d\ge 2$ and denote by  
\[
\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}:=\bigl\{(n_{1},\dots ,n_{d})\; ;\; n_{i}\in\mathbb{N}_{0}\bigr\}.
\]  
Fix a family of non-negative real numbers  
\[
\bigl(x_{n}\bigr)_{\,n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}}
\qquad\bigl(\text{with the convention }x_{\mathbf{0}}:=1\bigr)
\]  
satisfying

(1)  (sub-multiplicativity)  
\[
x_{m+n}\le x_{m}\,x_{n}\qquad\forall\,m,n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d};
\]

(2)  (non-degeneracy on the coordinate axes)  
for every unit vector $e_{i}$, $1\le i\le d$, the sequence $\bigl(x_{k e_{i}}\bigr)_{k\ge 1}$ is **not** eventually $0$;

(3)  (mild exponential upper bound)  
there exists a constant $C>0$ such that  
\[
x_{n}\le C^{\,\lVert n\rVert_{1}}\qquad\forall\,n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d},
\qquad\hbox{where }\lVert n\rVert_{1}:=n_{1}+\dots +n_{d}.
\]

For a rational direction $\theta=(\theta_{1},\dots ,\theta_{d})$ with $\theta_{i}\ge 0$ and
$\theta_{1}+\dots +\theta_{d}=1$, let $s$ be any common denominator of the $\theta_{i}$.  
Whenever $k\in s\mathbb{N}$ we put $k\theta:=(k\theta_{1},\dots ,k\theta_{d})\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$.

(a)  (directional limits)  
Show that the limit  
\[
L(\theta)\ :=\ \lim_{k\to\infty}x_{k\theta}^{\,1/k}
\]
exists (it may be $0$) for every rational direction $\theta$.  
Define  
\[
L_{i}\ :=\ \lim_{k\to\infty}x_{k e_{i}}^{\,1/k}\qquad(1\le i\le d).
\]

(b)  (asymptotic upper envelope)  
Prove that  
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\;=\;
\max_{1\le i\le d}L_{i}.
\tag{$\star$}
\]

(c)  (a necessary condition for a global limit)  
Show that if the global limit  
\[
\lim_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\]
exists, then necessarily $L_{1}=L_{2}=\dots =L_{d}$.

(d)  (failure of sufficiency)  
For $d=2$ construct an explicit family $\bigl(x_{n}\bigr)$ satisfying (1)-(3) with
$L_{1}=L_{2}$ but for which the global limit in (c) does **not** exist.
Explain why this shows that the condition found in (c) is not sufficient.

Throughout you may set $\log 0:=-\infty$ and work in the extended real line
$\mathbb{R}\cup\{-\infty\}$.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  
\[
b_{n}:=\log x_{n}\in\mathbb{R}\cup\{-\infty\},
\qquad\bigl(\log 0:=-\infty,\; \log 1 =0\bigr).
\]  
With the normalisation $x_{\mathbf{0}}=1$ we have $b_{\mathbf{0}}=0$.  
Assumptions (1) and (3) become

(S)\; $b_{m+n}\le b_{m}+b_{n}$  for all $m,n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$ (sub-additivity),  

(B)\; $b_{n}\le (\log C)\lVert n\rVert_{1}$ for all $n$ (linear upper bound).

Step 1.  Directional limits - part (a).  

Fix any non-zero $v\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$.  
The sequence $k\longmapsto b_{k v}$ is sub-additive, so by Fekete's lemma the limit  
\[
\beta(v):=\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{b_{k v}}{k}\in[-\infty,\infty)
\]  
exists.  
Given a rational $\theta$ choose the **primitive** integer vector $v$ with
$\theta=v/\lVert v\rVert_{1}$.  
If $k\in s\mathbb{N}$ then $k\theta=(k/s)v$, whence
\[
\frac{b_{k\theta}}{k}\;=\;\frac{b_{(k/s)v}}{(k/s)}\cdot\frac{1}{\lVert v\rVert_{1}}
\;\xrightarrow{k\to\infty}\;
\frac{\beta(v)}{\lVert v\rVert_{1}}.
\]
Exponentiating yields
\[
L(\theta)=\exp\!\Bigl(\beta(v)/\lVert v\rVert_{1}\Bigr).
\tag{1}
\]
In particular, for $v=e_{i}$ we get $L_{i}=e^{\,\beta(e_{i})}$.  
Thus the limits in (a) exist.  \hfill$\square$ (a)

Step 2.  Coordinate-wise decomposition.  

Claim.  For every $n=(n_{1},\dots ,n_{d})\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$ one has  
\[
b_{n}\le b_{n_{1}e_{1}}+\dots +b_{n_{d}e_{d}}.
\tag{2}
\]

Proof by induction on $m:=\lVert n\rVert_{1}$.  
For $m=0$ both sides equal $0$.  
Assume (2) is known for all vectors of 1-norm $<m$ and take
$n$ with $\lVert n\rVert_{1}=m\ge 1$.  
Choose an index $j$ with $n_{j}\ge 1$ and write
\[
n=(n_{1},\dots ,n_{j}-1,\dots ,n_{d})+e_{j}.
\]
Both addends are in $\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$, the first one having strictly smaller
1-norm.  Sub-additivity (S) gives
\[
b_{n}\le b_{n-e_{j}}+b_{e_{j}}
        \le\bigl[b_{(n_{1}e_{1})}+\dots +b_{(n_{j}-1)e_{j}}+\dots +b_{n_{d}e_{d}}\bigr]+b_{e_{j}}
        = b_{n_{1}e_{1}}+\dots +b_{n_{d}e_{d}},
\]
where the middle inequality uses the inductive hypothesis for $n-e_{j}$.  
Thus (2) holds for all $n$. \hfill$\square$

Step 3.  Proof of the asymptotic upper envelope - part (b).  

Let
\[
M_{\beta}:=\max_{1\le i\le d}\beta(e_{i})\quad\Bigl(=\log\!\bigl[\max_{i}L_{i}\bigr]\Bigr).
\]

(A)  The **finite** case $\;M_{\beta}>-\infty$.  

Fix $\varepsilon>0$.  
For each $i$ the limit $\beta(e_{i})$ exists, hence there are infinitely many
$k$ with $x_{k e_{i}}>0$ and
\[
b_{k e_{i}}\le k\bigl(M_{\beta}+\varepsilon/2\bigr).
\tag{3}
\]
Pick one such integer and denote it by $K_{i}\ge 1$.  
Define
\[
K_{\max}:=\max_{i}K_{i},
\qquad
M:=\max_{1\le i\le d}\;\max_{0\le r<K_{i},\;x_{r e_{i}}>0} b_{r e_{i}},
\]
which is finite by (B).  

Now let $n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{d}$ with $\lVert n\rVert_{1}\gg 1$.
Write $n_{i}=q_{i}K_{i}+r_{i}$ with $0\le r_{i}<K_{i}$.  
Using (2) and (3),
\[
\begin{aligned}
b_{n}\ &\le \sum_{i=1}^{d} b_{n_{i}e_{i}}
      \le \sum_{i=1}^{d}\bigl[q_{i}\,b_{K_{i}e_{i}}+b_{r_{i}e_{i}}\bigr]  \\
      &\le \sum_{i=1}^{d}q_{i}K_{i}\bigl(M_{\beta}+\varepsilon/2\bigr)+dM
      \le \lVert n\rVert_{1}\bigl(M_{\beta}+\varepsilon/2\bigr)+dM.
\end{aligned}
\]
Dividing by $\lVert n\rVert_{1}$ and letting $\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty$ gives
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}\frac{b_{n}}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\le M_{\beta}+\varepsilon.
\]
Because $\varepsilon>0$ is arbitrary,
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}\frac{b_{n}}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\le M_{\beta}.
\tag{4}
\]

(B)  The **degenerate** case $\;M_{\beta}=-\infty$ (equivalently $L_{i}=0$ for all $i$).  

Choose any positive number $M_{0}>0$.  
For every $i$ there exist infinitely many $k$ with $x_{k e_{i}}>0$ and
$b_{k e_{i}}\le -kM_{0}$.  Fix one such $k$ and call it $K_{i}$.  
Repeating the preceding argument with these $K_{i}$'s yields, for all large $n$,
\[
\frac{b_{n}}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}\le -M_{0}+\frac{dM}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\;<\;-M_{0}/2.
\]
Since $M_{0}>0$ was arbitrary we deduce
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}\frac{b_{n}}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
=-\infty.
\tag{5}
\]

(C)  Lower bound in the finite case.  
Take $n=k e_{j}$ for any index $j$ with $\beta(e_{j})=M_{\beta}$.  
Then $\displaystyle \lim_{k\to\infty}\tfrac{b_{k e_{j}}}{k}=M_{\beta}$, hence
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}\frac{b_{n}}{\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
\ge M_{\beta}.
\tag{6}
\]

Combining (4)-(6) and exponentiating we obtain in **all** cases  
\[
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
=\max_{1\le i\le d}L_{i},
\]
which is exactly $(\star)$. \hfill$\square$ (b)

Step 4.  Necessity of equal one-dimensional limits - part (c).  

Assume the global limit 
$L:=\displaystyle\lim_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}$
exists.  
Evaluating it along the $i$-th coordinate axis ($n=k e_{i}$) gives
\[
\lim_{k\to\infty}x_{k e_{i}}^{\,1/k}=L,
\quad\text{i.e. }L_{i}=L\qquad(1\le i\le d).
\]
Thus $L_{1}=L_{2}=\dots =L_{d}$. \hfill$\square$ (c)

Step 5.  A counter-example in dimension $2$ - part (d).  

For $n=(n_{1},n_{2})\ne\mathbf{0}$ set  
\[
x_{n}:=\begin{cases}
\mathrm{e}^{\,n_{1}+n_{2}} & \text{if }n_{1}=0\ \text{or}\ n_{2}=0,\\[4pt]
1                          & \text{if }n_{1},n_{2}>0,
\end{cases}
\qquad\text{and }x_{\mathbf{0}}:=1.
\tag{7}
\]

Verification of the assumptions.  

*  (1)  A direct case distinction using (7) shows  
$x_{m+n}\le x_{m}\,x_{n}$ for all $m,n\in\mathbb{N}_{0}^{2}$.  

*  (2)  Along either axis $x_{(k,0)}=x_{(0,k)}=\mathrm{e}^{\,k}>0$, hence the axis
sequences are not eventually $0$.  

*  (3)  For every $n$ one has $x_{n}\le\mathrm{e}^{\,\lVert n\rVert_{1}}$.  

Directional limits.  On the axes $x_{k e_{i}}^{\,1/k}=\mathrm{e}$, so
$L_{1}=L_{2}=\mathrm{e}$.  

Global behaviour.  Along the diagonal $n=(k,k)$ we have $x_{n}=1$, hence
$x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}=1$, while along the axis $n=(k,0)$ the same
quantity equals $\mathrm{e}$.  Therefore
\[
\liminf_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}
=1
\;<\;
\mathrm{e}
=
\limsup_{\lVert n\rVert_{1}\to\infty}x_{n}^{\,1/\lVert n\rVert_{1}}.
\]
The global limit fails to exist although $L_{1}=L_{2}$, so the condition found
in (c) is **not** sufficient.  \hfill$\square$ (d)

All four parts (a)-(d) are now complete. \qed
